paragraphs.body: The Melody of Warfare

His shoes scraped against the half-destroyed concrete of the stairs he was attempting to climb. The smell of death hung in the air, clouding his senses, vivid, nightmarish memories flashing behind his eyes. It had been months since he had last climbed these stairs. Seen this hallway. Looked at the door that was slightly off its hinges.

His breath stuttered in his chest. It came faster as his mind veered over every possible thing that could be behind that door. Would it be how he had left it? Dilapidated and demolished? Or was everything still in the place it had been when they walked out the door that morning?

annotations.comment: It’s a great opening — word choice could be stronger here and there, but overall, very absorbing. I don’t love the title, sorry!
annotations.context_left: in his chest. It came faster as his mind veered over every possible thing that could be behind that
annotations.context_right: . Would it be how he had left it? Dilapidated and demolished? Or was everything still in the place i

paragraphs.body: He took a step closer, heart pounding so hard in his ears that he could hear nothing else. Except for the melody floating from inside. It couldn’t be. Just like that, his panic reared its ugly head, and he pounded the rest of the way down the hall and threw the door open, and yet, the music didn’t falter for even a second.

annotations.comment: A small point but I couldn’t tell if he was running towards the apartment or away.

paragraphs.body: The inside of their flat took his breath away. Walls crumbled, furniture shattered, belongings thrown around every possible inch, and everything covered in a thick layer of dust. He stood in the doorway, unable to hear anything but that god forsaken melody and his own heart beating. He shook, whether with rage or fear, he was unsure.

annotations.comment: Another small point, but I’d go with “Crumbled walls…” here.

paragraphs.body: He shook his head, clearing away some of the images desperately trying to break free from the box he had shoved violently into the back of his mind. He stepped in further, over the plaster littering the floor, and went in search of the music.

annotations.comment: This sentence needs a little work — the use of “shook” again, and the metaphor of the box is slightly confusing. (Yes, these are very picky points — the draft is in good shape so far and just needs line edits!)

paragraphs.body: “Hello?” he called. “Is anyone here?”

annotations.comment: I wondered if he was able to be so clear about who might be there. Wouldn’t he perhaps think it was his family somehow?

paragraphs.body: There was always the chance that a squatter had made their way in for shelter.
Nobody responded.
The music got louder as he crept past the kitchen, much in the same state as the living room, and towards their bedroom. That had to be where the music was coming from.

He listened for breathing or movement but heard nothing. Not even the rats that had flooded every still-standing structure wandered about. Reaching the bedroom door, which was firmly shut, the music was loudest from within.

Nervously, he gripped the doorknob and gave the door a shove, but it didn’t open. He tried again to no avail. There must be something blocking it. Most likely a piece of the wall or ceiling that had been blown in.

annotations.comment: I wondered about how stable this apartment building is. Does he feel unsafe? Has he been told to keep away until now? And that brings me to a larger point to consider, which is “why today?” By which I mean, why is this particular day the day he went back for the first time?
annotations.context_left: Nervously, he gripped the doorknob and gave the door a shove, but it didn’t open. He tried aga
annotations.context_right: to no avail. There must be something blocking it. Most likely a piece of the wall or ceiling that h

paragraphs.body: So, with all his strength, he pushed and pushed, moving whatever it was inch by inch until he had an opening just big enough that he could squeeze through.

The first thing that caught his attention was the blaring TV in the corner. A home video was playing on the screen. Funny considering this part of town hadn’t had power in weeks. The melody that he had heard from the hallway echoed out from the speakers. It was interrupted by quick whispering.

annotations.comment: The implication here is of something otherworldly. If that’s what you want, you’re good.

paragraphs.body: “Dear future watchers. If this message is reaching you, then I, Emrys Black, am still living after disturbing my beautiful husband during one of his rehearsal sessions. He takes these matters very seriously. All of our neighbors complain about is how loud his violin is and how he plays at all hours of the day. But what they don’t know is, Malakai could wake me from death with the most putrid melody ever known to man, and I would still sit wherever he would allow me to in order to listen.”

annotations.text: Malakai could wake me from death with the most putrid melody ever known to man, and I would still sit wherever he would allow me to in order to listen.”
annotations.comment: This needs some rewording, hard to follow. Their names are a lot!

paragraphs.body: The camera panned past the man who spoke, through a crack in the door, to another who sat on a stool in the middle of the room. The violin in his hands continued the melody that had been overshadowed by the speaking.

annotations.comment: I want to have some help from our main character as to what I’m watching. He must recognize it, right? But the story has him silent here.

paragraphs.body: “Emrys, you know I can hear you recording on the other side of the door right,” Malakai spoke without missing a beat.
The camera whipped away; faceless laughter caught in blackness.
Then the video shuttered for a moment, the frames seeming to get caught. When it resumed, it began from the beginning of the many bars of melody.
He watched the video again. And again. And again. His hands trembled and his eyes leaked tears.

annotations.comment: Again, I want more here, to know what he thinks of seeing this — for the first time, or again?

paragraphs.body: When he couldn’t take it anymore, he stumbled from the bedroom, breath coming faster than he could control. His eyes were so glassy that he couldn’t see where he was going, falling over the broken furniture and plaster.

His feet tripped out from under him, and he collided violently with the ground, pain bursting from his face as blood rose to the surface. Nothing could stop the panic that wracked his body. He trembled as everything that he tried to push down came flooding back.

The blinding white light as they stepped out from the library. Then the gunfire and the chaos that followed. The look on Malakai’s face as they realized they were caught out in the open. The whistle that made his ears bleed.

annotations.comment: I’m not a PTSD expert, but I wonder if watching that video would trigger him? If so, why was he able to watch it so many times? Might it work better to have an explosion, crash or flash of light set him off after watching it?

paragraphs.body: “Run Emrys! You have to run faster,” Malakai screamed. His husband was practically dragging him down the street.

He heard the screams all around him, smelling the copper tang of fresh blood from the dead bodies littering the road. They had been safe. Just moments ago, they were safe. It was all he could hear in his head.
Emrys could do nothing but be pulled as fast as he could go by Malakai. He couldn’t even tell where they were, let alone where they needed to go. He just had to follow.
Malakai stumbled in front of him, falling to the ground. Emrys was there in a second, grabbing his arm and pulling him up.
“Malakai, come on, we have to go!”
But where? They were too far from their apartment, and he was too discombobulated to tell which way the bomb shelter was. Bombs always followed the gunfire. It was only a matter of time.

annotations.comment: Might be helpful to let us know what they were out doing.

paragraphs.body: His body trembled as he met his husband’s eyes and saw the fear there. Emrys looked around as the sound of bullets grew closer to where they were standing in the middle of the street. Soon they would be whizzing past their faces.
“What do we do,” he begged. They couldn’t die here. Not when everything had been fine just moments ago.
Malakai grabbed his hand. “We have to get off of the street.”

Emrys looked around for the nearest open building, praying that the soldiers weren’t going door to door like he heard they’d done in other towns. Malakai must have spotted something before him because in seconds he was once again being dragged through the treacherous roads toward the only place that may guarantee their lives. He heard the boots of the soldiers coming closer to them and saw as they turned the corner, firing in their direction.

annotations.comment: I like the drama of it and I appreciate that he is not sure of what to do and needs help.
annotations.context_right: nearest open building, praying that the soldiers weren’t going door to door like he heard they’d do

paragraphs.body: They ducked through the doorway, Emrys’s head whipping around to try and figure out where they were.

Rows and rows of vacant sewing machines lined the abandoned warehouse floor. The sweatshop. Of course. He should have recognized the building from the distinctive blue doorway they had just come through. He walked past here every morning on his way to get coffee and bread.

annotations.comment: Again, helpful to know what they were out doing. I assume you don’t want to identify the country where this is taking place but locating us in the setting a bit more will help.

paragraphs.body: “We have to get as far inside this warehouse as possible,” Malakai spoke as they rushed across the floor. “It has to be deep enough in to hopefully withstand the bombs and be vacant enough that the soldiers won’t notice we slipped inside and try to follow us.”

annotations.comment: Lots of information to get out while also running!

paragraphs.body: He was always so level-headed. Even when things were literally about to fall from the sky, he always had a plan. But if there was anything I knew about my husband, it was this, he was terrified.

annotations.comment: First person

paragraphs.body: They hurried through the winding halls, going down staircase after staircase to get as far away from the ground level as possible. Hopefully nothing would reach them down here. The air was damp and smelled of dust bunnies and moss. Malakai quickly guided him into a room he hadn’t even noticed.
“We’ll wait here until after the bombs. It should be safe enough. Or as safe as we can be without being in a locked bomb shelter.”

Emrys gave him a half-hearted smile as he surveyed their surroundings. Everything was covered in cobwebs. There were some long forgotten Christmas decorations in the corner, along with some probably busted sewing machines. A desk was pressed against one wall and the opposite one was lined with filing cabinets. This would have to do.
He crossed the room and practically fell onto the floor to sit. He still hadn’t stopped shaking. Malakai sat beside him, taking his hand from his lap and grasping it firmly.
“It’s going to be ok. We’re going to be just fine. In a few hours, we’ll go back to the apartment and fix dinner. I’ll even play the violin for you after.”

annotations.comment: Assign this dialogue. I actually thought Emrys was the player, so this confused me! A good reason to clarify that section earlier.

paragraphs.body: Malakai always played to help calm him after the bombings. Emrys looked at him, studying the worry on his face.
“If we still have an apartment,” he said.
Malakai chuckled quietly.
He pressed himself into his husband’s side, getting as comfortable as possible. They would be here for a while.
The hours passed in silence. He couldn’t hear the gunshots anymore.
The silence persisted.
No bombs fell.
His eyes grew tired. He fought to stay awake, but it was a fight he wouldn’t win. Emrys mumbled something incoherently to Malakai before he let himself drift into sleep.
When he woke, it had to be well after sunset. Drool was dried on his face and all his muscles ached from his uncomfortable position on Malakai’s lap. He must have shifted in his sleep.

He brushed the back of his hand across the face, attempting to wipe away the saliva but it only smeared. He pulled his hand away and made to look at it, but the sleep still hadn’t cleared from his eyes and the darkness was almost all consuming.
He blinked a few times until his vision came into focus. Red. The back of his hand was red.

annotations.comment: It’s very unnerving!

paragraphs.body: He shifted around to ask Malakai if he was seeing things. Red soaked the front of Malakai’s shirt. When Emrys looked to his face, his eyes were open, staring at nothing, blood trickling out of the corner of his mouth.
“Malakai?”
He pushed himself out of his husband’s lap and knelt in front of him.
“Malakai,” he asked again.
His hands reached for his husband, grabbing onto his face. The skin was freezing. Fear washed over him.
“Malakai, wake up!”

His hands scrambled over his husband’s body, searching for the source of the large blood stain on the front of his shirt. He yanked up the hem and beheld the bullet hole going through his abdomen. How had he not noticed this before? Why didn’t Malakai say anything?
“Malakai,” he plead as he shook his husband’s shoulders.
His body was limp under his hands. Tears streamed down Emrys’s face, breath catching in this throat.
“Malakai-” his voice faltered. “You have to wake up baby. We have to go home. Please wake up.”
When nothing but the sound of his own sobs echoed back at him, he shattered, screaming into the chest of his dead husband.

annotations.comment: It’s very effective. But I would actually like to get some insight into how Malakai likely died. It is a little hard to believe he was shot (?) and Emrys never noticed.

paragraphs.body: Emrys clawed at his own chest as the memory played over and over. He couldn’t breathe. He couldn’t feel his body. Why did he ever come back here?

He laid on the floor of their tattered apartment until the sobs slowed and his breath evened back out. Slowly, when he felt like he wouldn’t shatter again, he pulled himself from the floor and brushed some of the dust and rubble from his clothes. He swiped at the blood trickling down his face, the same way he did that day.

He had come for one thing. The video had been an unintentional setback. He was watching it that morning and had left it on the TV player. It must have kicked back on once the power grid came back up. Even though every building didn’t have power yet, theirs must have.

annotations.comment: This explanation felt a little forced to me. I had trouble buying he thought of it right then. Felt more like the author needing to insert an explanation.
annotations.context_left: was watching it that morning and had left it on the TV player. It must have kicked back on once the
annotations.context_right: grid came back up. Even though every building didn’t have power yet, theirs must have.

paragraphs.body: He shuffled across the living room, looking at the life that had been torn from his hands. Malakai’s piles of books had been scattered across the floor. Emrys’s coffee cup lay broken in pieces besides the coffee table. Everything had been perfect that morning months ago.

They had gone out for breakfast together, but not after Emrys drank his morning coffee while Malakai had played his scales in their bedroom. Malakai said that if they were going to spend the day galivanting around town then he at least had to get some practice in.

annotations.comment: This, too, feels like the wrong moment to share this information. I wondered why exactly he went home today.
annotations.context_left: They had gone out for breakfast to
annotations.context_right: her, but not after Emrys drank his morning coffee while Malakai had played his scales in their bedro

paragraphs.body: Emrys bent down to drag a particularly large section of the wall out of the way of the closet door with great struggle. It took all the energy he had left in his body. He pulled the door open.

There, despite all the dust, was Malakai’s violin case. Emrys pulled it from within, brushing off the debris. He kicked some of the rubble and their belongings out of his way and crouched to the floor, placing the case in front of him.

annotations.comment: Is this what he specifically comes back for?
annotations.context_left: There, despite all the dust, was Malakai’s viol
annotations.context_right: case. Emrys pulled it from within, brushing off the debris. He kicked some of the rubble and their

paragraphs.body: Carefully, he popped the clasps and lifted the lid. Inside and perfectly untouched, was the violin. He could smell Malakai’s cologne on the fabric that lined the case. The sheet music stuffed into the lid pocket was scribbled with his handwriting. The bow was clipped in its place, and behind it, a photo he had never known of was there.
He pulled it out and had to keep from spiraling all over again. Despite how protective of his practice time and how much he hated being recorded; the photo was of Emrys.
It was the picture Malakai had taken of him when he wasn’t looking at their wedding day. Emrys had flowers pinned into his hair and what bits of his suit you could see was covered in stars.

annotations.comment: I love this image. I wondered if Emrys has ever seen it before?

paragraphs.body: He felt the tears well in his eyes, and they fell before he could stop them. He had avoided coming back here for months because of this. Because he could still hear his husband’s melodies in his head, feel his breath against his cheek and hear his voice when he closed his eyes.

annotations.comment: Which again makes me want to know why he did go back this day?

paragraphs.body: He turned the photo over, and scrawled across the back was ‘For my husband, Emrys, who doesn’t know that he is the inspiration for all of my music’.

annotations.comment: I didn’t quite buy that inscription on a picture Malakai was carrying around *of* Emrys.

paragraphs.body: Emrys cried as he placed the photo back, closed the case, and picked it up. Then he hurried into the bedroom and took the tape from the TV. If he couldn’t have his husband, then his melodies would help him still feel close to Malakai.

Standing back in the doorway to their apartment, he surveyed what was left of their life. The books and sheet music. The fashion sketches Emrys had brought home from work and their matching coffee cups.
All of it broken or tattered in some way. But this wasn’t his home anymore. Malakai was his home. He was dead. His husband was dead because of the stupid war. And Emrys would never be the same.
So, when he walked from that apartment, he didn’t look back. He just gripped the violin case and prayed. Prayed that soon, he would get to see his husband again.

annotations.comment: As mentioned, this last line doesn’t quite work for me. I hadn’t any sense of a sort of religious outlook until here so it feels a little flat. Strong draft, Emery! I really enjoyed reading it. My notes are mostly about clarifying small sections so that the story works, but I do think you have a large issue to resolve, which will affect the story deeply — that is, why did Emrys return on this particular day and what was his intention in doing so? You could decide that this is their wedding anniversary. Or it could be the day before the building is torn down. Or it could be the first day of spring. I don’t know! It’s your call, but that really affects everything else, so choose wisely! Happy revising!
